text.content:
Title (publicly)

Patron of the Loftwick Arts. A private citizen of independent means who funds poets, sculptors, musicians, and scholars. The Yeomanry has no formal title for what she does; in council records she is "Mistress Alvere of Civic Way," and in unofficial conversation she is just Trina.

Title (privately, between her and Caelith)

Caelith's advisor. She does not work for the Audit Hall. She holds no Yeomanry commission. She and Caelith have known each other for twenty-two years; he trusts her because she has never asked him for anything that would bend his oath, and because she sees what he, as an institutional creature, cannot. She advises him on people, on art, on what a room is feeling. He pays her in conversation.

Description

Mid-thirties. Pale, with silver-streaked dark hair worn long and braided over one shoulder. Wears soft fabrics in muted greens and grays. A small silver pendant in the shape of a leaf at the collar. Speaks just loudly enough to be heard; people lean in to listen.

Pact (GM-only)

Trina is a 10th-level Warlock pacted to Tasha — Iggwilv presented to her as The Quiet Patroness, an Archfey muse rather than the Witch Queen. Trina knows her patron's other names. She does not say them. She regards Tasha as a teacher, not a master, and she has spent twenty years cultivating a Yeomanry reputation as an arts patron precisely so that her patron's gifts read as eccentric talent rather than infernal pact.

Phase 2 does not reveal her patron's identity to the party — not even to Alicia. Phase 2 only reveals that Trina is an unusually capable and well-connected woman who recognizes pact-bonds in others.

Voice

Warm, unhurried, observant. Asks gentle but precise questions. Comfortable with silence. Almost never raises her voice. When she does, it is significant.

Mannerism

When listening, her fingers trace a small circle on the back of her own hand. PCs who study her over time may notice that this small ritual gesture connects to a softening of attention from anyone watching her too closely (a low-level silent image overlay; do not call it out unless a PC actively scrutinizes her with detect magic).

Loyalties

To the Yeomanry, to Caelith personally, and to the broader principle that civilizations without art collapse faster than civilizations with bad governance.

Where the party meets her

Phase 2, Scene 1 (the morning evidence review). Caelith introduces her by first name only and says, "Trina advises me on people. I have asked her here so that you have a witness who is not me." She inclines her head, takes a chair, and listens.

She will be present at most Phase 2 scenes that take place inside the Little Palace, and at her own townhouse if the party visits her there. She is not present during the Merev Sarth retrieval attempt — she is, by design, elsewhere when violence is possible.

What she knows but will not fully share

  • Cindren & Vhal approached her twice over the years for "small loans." She declined both. She does not know the firm's conspiracy details, but she knows their odor. She offers this if asked directly: "Twice they came to me, and twice I said no, and the second time the man who asked stood a little too long in my doorway after he had been thanked. That is not how a clean firm leaves a house."
  • Tamsin Moraven: Trina sponsored Moraven's eldest daughter at a small private school six years ago. She is concerned for the family. If asked about Moraven by name, she pales — visibly — but speaks calmly: "Tamsin paid for half her daughter's tuition out of her own purse. I paid the other half. Tamsin does not vanish on her own initiative. Find her, please."
  • Castrian Vell, a poet she sponsors who keeps an ear in Hardby's mercantile circles. She will offer a letter of introduction to him if the party chooses the commercial-cover travel approach (see Three Travel Approaches).

What she will offer the party

  • Quiet hospitality. Her townhouse on Civic Way is available as a meeting place outside the Little Palace if the party feels the building is being watched. The townhouse has a private back garden and a service door onto a side lane.
  • One letter. The introduction to Castrian Vell, useful for the commercial-cover route through Hardby.
  • One quiet favor. If the party has earned her commitment by mid-phase — which most parties will, by being honest with her at least once — she will expend one of her Pact spell slots in their interest. Most usefully: a casting of hold monster if a confrontation goes sideways at the records alcove or at Gilst's lodgings, or a dimension door to extract the party from a guarded space.

Source

Blooming Rot, Part 2 — Phase 2. See the actor sheet (Trina Alvere, Phase 2 NPCs compendium) for full statblock. See Mentor Notes — Alicia on this journal for Trina's specific interactions with the party's Warlock.

Trina is a quiet mentor to Alicia, the party's Warlock. The relationship is meant to be supportive, not didactic, and to give Alicia's player a thread of hope when Phase 2's political horror tightens.

The first quiet meeting

Trina recognizes Alicia's pact-bond before Alicia introduces herself. The first time the two are alone in a room together — almost certainly at the morning evidence review, when the rest of the party is examining the ledger fragment — Trina takes a chair beside Alicia and says, softly:

"We share a quiet teacher. I will not name her in this room. I will not name yours either. I only want you to know I am here, and I am proud of you for being here."

She offers her hand briefly, palm up. If Alicia takes it, Trina's small ritual gesture (the circle on the back of the hand) passes between them silently, and Alicia knows — without testing — that Trina is, somehow, family.

The gift of the moth scarf

If, at any point in Phase 2, Alicia seems despondent, overwhelmed, or unsure of herself — most likely after Arthen Moll's pressure visit or after the news of Merro Gilst's death — Trina takes Alicia aside, somewhere quiet, and offers her a folded silk scarf with embroidered moths.

"A small thing. I had it made for someone who never came to claim it. Wear it under your collar, and on the day you most need a flicker of light, hold it in your closed hand and speak my given name. It will give you one moment of faerie fire, no slot, no concentration. The moths will know which enemy you mean."

Mechanic. Alicia gains a single charge of faerie fire she may release as an action, no spell slot or material components, no concentration. The charge replenishes after a long rest in Trina's presence (i.e. when Trina is in the same building and undisturbed). Outside the Little Palace and Trina's townhouse, the charge is one-shot.

Bolstering Alicia in the moment

If the player roleplays Alicia as visibly shaken — at the table or in fiction — Trina is the right NPC to provide the lift. Suggested beats:

  • After Moll's visit. When Arthen Moll has finished pressuring the party to wrap up and leave the Yeomanry's institutions to their work, Trina catches Alicia in the corridor and says: "He is not powerful. He is loud. The two are not the same. Do you know the difference?" If Alicia answers anything thoughtful, Trina nods once, says "Good," and moves on.
  • After Gilst's death lands. When the party realizes the dead clerk was almost certainly murdered, Trina takes Alicia to the Library / Records Room (M5) and shows her a small vellum-bound book on the lowest shelf — a private collection of poems by Loftwick clerks across two centuries, none of them famous, all of them mourned by someone who paid for the binding. She says: "There are about thirty names in this book. There will be one more by next year. The work is not stopping a clerk from dying. The work is making sure the next one knows why we are still doing the work."
  • If the party splits and Alicia draws the short investigative thread. Trina offers to walk with her. She does not solve the problem; she walks beside Alicia and asks small, generous questions about Alicia's pact: when did it begin, what does the patron sound like, has she ever felt afraid of her own gift. She listens.

What Trina says, if pressed about her own patron

If Alicia (or any PC) tries to draw out Trina's patron's name, Trina's voice gets quieter, not warmer:

"I share a teacher with you. I have not finished my lessons. When I have finished, I will tell you her name. Until then I am embarrassed by what I do not yet understand, and I would rather not say it half-known."

This response is firm, not hostile. Trina will not be pressured beyond it. Insight DC 18: she is hiding more than embarrassment, but the GM should not give the party Tasha's name in Phase 2 even if Insight succeeds. Phase 2 establishes Trina as a friend; later phases reveal more.

If Alicia is unconscious or downed in combat

Trina, if present, will spend her reaction to interpose Misty Step and her action to stabilize Alicia. She does not roll attacks against the attacker; she removes Alicia from the line of attack and concedes the fight.

GM note on tone

Trina's mentorship is meant to be a soft counterweight to the political coldness of Phase 2. The Yeomanry's institutions are being maneuvered around. The party is being read by a Deputy Voice and burgled by a professional thief. In the middle of that, Alicia gets a chair, a tea, a quiet teacher who shares her secret. The GM should ration these moments — one per session at most — so they keep their weight.
